nativeRange.habitat: Shallow coral reefs, weed and rubble areas, and seagrass beds typically at 2–15 m depth

diet.notes: In the wild, has been documented feeding on amphipods, polychaetes, and molluscs, plus algae and benthic invertebrates. Widely sold for its tendency to pick at Aiptasia and Majano nuisance anemones in reef tanks. Generally accepts a variety of prepared and frozen marine foods in captivity — mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, finely chopped krill/squid/shrimp/clam, quality marine pellets, and frozen marine-algae preparations — though some specimens are initially shy feeders and may need whole or live food to be coaxed onto prepared diets. Several small meals per day are generally advised.

goodWith: small to medium peaceful reef fish

imageCandidates.notes: Rickard Zerpe, 4608x3456. Sharp in-situ shot among anemone tentacles; whole fish framed, clearly shows the diagnostic mottled brown/white camouflage with dark bars, erect dorsal spine, bristly caudal fin and green eye. No watermark.

BJ Smit, 2048x1365. Vibrant in-situ reef shot; excellent detail of the matted/bristly body fringes and orange-brown mottling with white speckling. Light photographer signature watermark along the bottom edge.
Gorbunova M.S., 5148x3437. Clean high-res aquarium profile against blue water; sharp, whole fish framed, dorsal spine and bristly caudal visible. Different individual/setting for variety.

summary: A small Indo-Pacific filefish popular for its taste for Aiptasia and Majano nuisance anemones. Generally hardy and peaceful, but commonly considered reef-safe only with caution — individuals have been known to nip soft corals, zoanthids, and feather dusters.
careNotes:
The Aiptasia Eating Filefish (also widely sold as Bristletail or Matted Filefish) is a small, somewhat cryptic member of the family Monacanthidae, found on shallow Indo-West Pacific reefs, weed beds, and rubble zones. It is most often purchased not as a display fish in its own right but as a biological control for Aiptasia and Majano anemone outbreaks in reef tanks. Most specimens do develop a taste for these pest anemones, though individual results vary — some fish ignore Aiptasia entirely — so a backup chemical or manual removal plan is generally advised.

Reef compatibility is the species' most important caveat. The Aiptasia Eating Filefish is broadly regarded as reef-safe with caution: once Aiptasia is exhausted, many individuals have been reported to begin sampling soft corals, zoanthids, polyps, feather dusters, and small ornamental shrimp. Aquarists with prized colonies of softies or zoanthids should monitor closely and be prepared to remove the fish if nipping begins. SPS-dominated tanks (those housing stony, hard-skeleton corals rather than soft corals) have been reported to fare better than mixed or soft-coral-heavy systems.

The species is generally considered peaceful toward unrelated tankmates but commonly territorial toward its own kind and other filefish, so a single specimen per tank is the standard recommendation unless the system is very large. Plenty of rockwork with crevices to retreat into is important, along with open swimming space — the species is an active, curious swimmer that has been reported to spend much of its time picking through rocks for food. A varied diet is generally advised: in addition to the anemones it will graze on naturally, offer mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, finely chopped seafood, and marine-algae preparations several times daily.

Whether a given specimen will reliably consume Aiptasia is not predictable in advance; the behavior is common enough in the species to make it a worthwhile biological control option, but it is not guaranteed in every individual.

This species is also sold under the retail name 'Clown Filefish,' which is used interchangeably with Aiptasia Eating Filefish, Bristletail Filefish, and Matted Filefish — all referring to the same species, Acreichthys tomentosus, with no distinct color morph or husbandry difference.
breedingNotes: Oviparous in the wild. Captive breeding is uncommonly reported and is not considered an established hobby project; the species is overwhelmingly traded as wild-caught specimens.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Collection landing page used to confirm common-name aliases (Aiptasia Eating / Bristletail / Matted Filefish), scientific name Acreichthys tomentosus, and the general LiveAquaria filefish husbandry guidance (adequate hiding plus ample swimming space). The species-specific LiveAquaria product page was found via search at /product/2562 and used for the detailed husbandry figures: 30 US gal minimum, 5.5 in max, 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12, care level Moderate, peaceful temperament except toward conspecifics, omnivore diet, reef-safe with caution due to coral nipping.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-confirmed scientific name Acreichthys tomentosus (Linnaeus, 1758), family Monacanthidae, order Tetraodontiformes, max length 14.0 cm SL, depth 2–15 m, marine reef-associated/non-migratory, Indo-West Pacific distribution (Sri Lanka to New Caledonia, north to southern Japan), wild temperature range 22–28°C (modelled preferred 25.4–29.1°C), solitary behavior, diet of amphipods/polychaetes/molluscs, oviparous reproduction, IUCN Least Concern.

additional.name: Bulk Reef Supply
additional.notes: Used as additional secondary because the Reef2Reef wiki page for this species returned HTTP 403. Cross-checked 30 US gal minimum, 75–80°F (23–27°C), pH 7.7–8.5, SG 1.020–1.026, omnivore behavior with supplemental feeding required, caveat that not all individuals consume Aiptasia, peaceful curious temperament, and the reef-safety variability (may nip soft corals, feather dusters, small crustaceans).

fish.breedingNotes: Oviparous in the wild. No established captive-breeding protocol; the species is essentially always wild-caught in the trade.
